danger_signals.visual_form: Industrial/research compound architecture across multiple themed stages
danger_signals.meaning: Enemy territory controlled by antagonist faction; player must infiltrate and destroy

danger_signals.visual_form: Mechanical enemy sprites distinct from organic threats
danger_signals.meaning: Primary danger source—weaponized military threat justifying the covert mission

danger_signals.visual_form: Water, ceilings, vertical shafts requiring suit adaptation
danger_signals.meaning: Themed facility dangers that gate progression and require power-suit acquisition

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible items obtained after defeating mid-level bosses
safety_signals.meaning: Progression checkpoints and capability unlocks that make player safer in subsequent challenges
Safe navigation spaces where player can move freely without immediate threat

faction_indicators.faction: U.S. Department of Defense / Player allegiance
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: NOVA protagonist identity; 'Patriot Suit' nomenclature suggests US military branding
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Mission framing as government-sanctioned covert operation; December 24, 2200 briefing context

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Cyborg soldiers, mechanized enemies, research facility aesthetics
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Industrial/weapons research compound architecture across five numbered Areas; final fortress level

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multiple facility stages remain intact; bosses active in each Area
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies patrol and defend their installations; stage hazards fully operational

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Mid-level bosses defeated, power suits acquired
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Destroyed installations gradually reduced; player character gains suit-specific visual cosmetics
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player gains stage-specific abilities (wall-climbing, swimming, flight, defense); subsequent areas become traversable

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Area 5 unlocked after first four bosses defeated
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Progression gate lifted; final fortress becomes accessible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Escalated enemy difficulty; final confrontation sequence triggered

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Game end state; all facilities eliminated

environmental_storytelling.example: Each of five Areas represents different Delta Foundation research/weapons divisions; environment type (water stage, sky stage, etc.) implies functional purpose
December 24, 2200 date stamp; U.S. President pressure from Delta Foundation's week-long ultimatum; threat of hostile government acquisition drives mission urgency

Collectible suits (Newt/wall-climbing, Wet/swimming, Rocket/flight, Patriot/defense) unlock stage-specific areas, implying Delta Foundation built redundant defenses requiring specialized counter-equipment

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Defend facility zones; defeat triggers power-suit drop and Area progression
Standard enemy encounters throughout stages; mechanized movement patterns imply programmed AI threat
Standard platformer mobility; restricted vertical and environmental access
Enhanced mobility tied to suit type; wall-climbing, swimming, flight, or defensive capabilities enable new stage routes
Escalated threat level; culmination of Delta Foundation's cyborg development program

notes: Power Blade 2 communicates world-state primarily through environmental theming and mechanical gate-keeping via power suits. The political situation (U.S. government vs. Delta Foundation weapons monopoly threat) is established through narrative framing rather than visual manifestation. Faction identity relies on contextual mission briefing and protagonist association with U.S. Department of Defense. Danger is signaled through stage architecture requiring suit-specific traversal and enemy density. The game uses stage-select freedom (Mega Man-style) to allow player agency while maintaining progression gates. Regional differences (JP 'Captain Saver' vs. US 'Power Blade 2' title) suggest localization but sources lack detail on visual communication differences.
